our File No. I -9 0-68 6
Dear Mr. Rodriguez:
This is in response to your letter requesting informal
written assistancel/ on behalf of the city of orange and the
orange Redevelopment Agency regarding the conf lict-of-interest
provisions of tire polifical 
Reiorm a-t ltne rraqt'rr;.2/
P1ease note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct that has already taken pIace. Moreover,
the Conmission has no jurisdiction over other provisions of the
Iaw which may have some application to your facts, such as
Government code section 1090 and the doctrine of incompatible
offices or activities. 
You should contact the Attorney General's
office for advice with respect to these areas of law.
February 8′  ■99■
OUESTIONS
rs an attorney retained by a redevelopment agency as
council to advise the agency on all Iega1 matters a
officialtt under the Act?
Does the special counsel, retained by the redevelopment
for all matters have a conffict of interest if he or his
1.
special
'rpubl ic
2.
agency
L/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
imnunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(section 83114,' Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
2/ covernment Code Sectj.ons 810oO-91-015. AlL statutory
references are to the covernment code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulatj-ons appear at 2 California Code of Requlations
Section 18000, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
428 J Strect,Suite 800 ● P.oo Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804‐ 0807 ● (916)322・5660


Our Fi■e No. ■-90-686
Page 2
employer al-so contracts with the agency to serve as bond counsel
for a particular bond issuance?
CONCLUSIONS
1. An attorney retained by a redevelopment agency as special
counsel to provide general advice and assistance to the agency on
an ongoing basis is a public official.
2. The special counsel s/ould be required to disqualify from
any decision which rrould foreseeably and materially affect his or
hei econornic interests, including decisions which wilL materially
affect the special counsel's own income and assets, the special
counsel's enployer, and clients of the special counsel's employer
if the specill counsel's ownership interest in his or her employer
is greatLr than 1ot and pro-rata share of the income from the
client is $250 or more.
FACTS
The city of orange has established the orange RedeveloPment
Agency (the iagency") in order to Plan the various redevelopment
piole-cti in the ciLy. The agency currently. contracts with
striating, 
Yocca, carlson and Rauth (the t'f irmrr) to serve as
special 6ounsel to the agency. one of the partners of the firm
uit= ." special counsel. The partner attends board meetings of
the agenc! and advj.ses and represents the agency on all legal
rnatteis plrtaining to redevelopment larr. The special counsel also
advises Lhe agency regarding the bond issuances the agency puts
forth to fund the redevelopnent projects-
The specJ.al counsel has tendered a contract requesting
selection of tfre firm as the agency's bond counsel for the bond
issuance. As bond counsel, the firn would advise the agency on
aII aspects of the rnunicipal finance offering being contenplated
and would be paid a contingency fee based on whether the bonds are
ultimately issued. As Senior Assistant City Attorney for the city
of orange you are seeking guidance with respect to advising the
special- counsel about potential conflicts of interest.
ANALYSI S
I. 
consultants
section 87100 prohibits any public official 
frorn making,
participating in rnaking, or otherwise usj.ng his official 
position
to influence a governmental decision in which the official 
has a
financial. interest. 
A rrpublic officiaf i is defined in Section
82048 to include every nenber, officer, enptoyee or consultant of
a state or loca1 governnent agency. Regulation 18700 (copy
enclosed) defines rtconsultanttr as follows:
rrconsuLtantrr shal1 include any natural person
who provides under contract, information, advice,


Our File No. 
■-90-686
Page 3
recommendation or counsel to a state or loca]
goverrunent aqJency, provided, ho$rever, that
rrconsultantrr shal1 not include a person who:
(A) conducts research and arrives at
conclusj.ons with respect to his or her rendi-
tion of j.nformation, advice, recommendation,
or counsel independent of control and direc-
tion of the agency or any agency official,
other than normal contract monitoring; and
(B) Possesses no authority with resPect
to any agency decision beyond the rendition of
information, advice, recommendation or
counsel,
3/ A rrconsultant[ as defined in Regulation 18700(a) (2) must be a
naturaL person and cannot be a business entity. 
Consequently, the
firm is not a trconsultant for purposes of the Act. 
(Russell
Advice Letter, No. A-88-484, copy enclosed.)
4/ The bond counsel might also qualify as a consultant under the
Act. You have not presented this question to us in your letter;
consequently, we have not addressed it.
5/ fn addition to disqual i fication responsibi l ities, consultants
to the agency are required to file Statements of Economic Inter-
ests pursuant to the agency,s conflict of j-nterest code.
Regulation 18700 (a) (2).
we have said that attorneys that advise an agency on an on-
going basis are consultants under the Act.r/ 
(Moe Advice Letter,
llo. i-Ag-+Sa; Gifford Advice Letter, No. A-85-201, copies
enclosed.) Hov.Iever, where an attorney is hired by an agency for a
single case, the attorney would not become a public officiaL.
(HoefIing Advice Letter, No. r-87-246, copy enclosed.)
According to your facts, the special counsel acts as the
agency's attorney, much as a city attorney acts as the attorney
for a-city. 
The special counsel provides information, advice,
reconrnendation and counsel to the agency lrith respect to all
natters t'that require legal analysis or opinion." Thus, it would
appear the special counsel will be a trconsuLtanttr as defined in
tie act, and a public official-. {/
ff. 
Econornic Interests
As a public official, 
the special counsel is prohibited from
naking, participating in naking, or otherwise using his or her
official 
positj.on to influence a goverrl4ental decision in which
the off ic-ia1 has a financial inteiest.5/ 
(Regulations 1870o and
18700.1, copies enclosed. ) Please be aware that unlike other


Our Fi■e No. ■-90-686
Page 4
provisions of the 1aw, the Act $rould not prohibit an individual
from holding any specific office. 
The conflict of interest
provisions are applied on a decision by decision basis and where a
decision $ril,1 affect an official's 
economic interest in a material
way, the off j,cial may not participate.
section I71o3 provides:
An off ici,al- has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision wilL
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the
official 
or a member of his or her inmediate farnily
or on3
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investrnent worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
***
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than toans by a conmercial lending
institution in the reguLar course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
doLlars ($25o) or more in value provided to,
received by or prornised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the tine when the
decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
off icj.al is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of rnanagement.
***
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investrnent or
interest ohrned by the spouse or dependent chil.d of
a public offieial, 
by an agent on behaLf of a
public official, 
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, 
the official's 
agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 1O-percent interest
or greater.
Presumably, the special counsel has received $250 or more in
incorne frorn his private sector employer, the f irrn. Thus the firn
is a source of income to the special counsel.
Further, as a partner/ o\,rner, the special counsel \.ri11 also
have an investment interest in the f irrn. Moreover, Section 82030


Our File No. 
■-90-686
Page 5
provides that the income of an individual also includes a oro-rata
share of anv incorne of anv business entitv or trust in which the
individual or spouse owns, directlv. indirectlv or beneficiallv, 
a
1o-percent interest or oreater. 
(Russell Advice Letter, supra.)
Thus, if the special counsel has a 1ot interest or greater in the
f irrn, any source of incorne to the firm within the last 12 months
which paid the firm enough to make the special counsel's Pro-rata
share $250 or more is potentially a disqualifying source of income
to the counsel .
consequently, the special counsel appears to have potentially
disqualifying eeonomic interests in decisions which affect any of
the fol lowing:
1. The personal expenses, income, assets (other than
interests in real property), or liabilities 
of the counsel;
2. The firm; and,
3. Clients of the firrn if the counsel's interest i,s greater
than 1ot and his pro-rata share of the income from the client is
$250 or more.
An official's 
economic interest is disqualifying with respect
to a specific governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision $ri1f have a rnaterial financiaf
effect, distinguishable frorn its effect on the public grenerally,
on the official 
or on his econonic interest.
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. certainty is not
required. However, if an effect is only a nere possibility, 
it is
not reasonabJ.y foreseeable. (fn re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops.
r-98 . )
III. 
Economic Interests Directlv Involved
In addition, the foreseeable effect on a source of income
must be material to require disqualification. 
The commission has
adopted differing guidelines to determine whether an effect is
rnaterial, depending on the specific circumstances of each
decision. Where the econonic interest is directly before the
official,s 
aerency, as an applicant or the subject of the decision,
Regulation 18702,1(a) (copy enclosed) provides that the effect of
the decision is generally deemed naterial . 
(Combs Advice Letter,
No. A-89-L77, copy enclosed. )
Regulation LA702.1 (b) provides:
(b) A person or business entity is directly
involved in a decision before an official,s 
agency


Our File No. 
■-90-686
Page 6
r.rhen that person or entity, either personally or by
an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which
the decision will be made by filing 
an
applicati-on, c1aim, appeal, or simil,ar request
ori
(2) Is a naned party in, or is the
subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official- or the official's
a9ency.
(3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves
the issuance, renerrral, apProval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlenent to, or contract with, the subject
person or business entity.
consequently, in many cases it appears that the special
counsel's econornic interest in the firm would be directly involved
and could result in a confLict of interest. 
For example, if the
decision involved the renewal or rnodification of the bond
counsel's contract or eval,uation of the bond counsel's services or
opinions, the firm would be directly j.nvolved and disqualif ication
is required.
Iv. 
Direct Effects on The official
In addition, a conflict of interest may exist where a
decision ti1I result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or
Iiabilities 
of the special counsel or his or her irnmediate fanily
increasing or decreasing by S250, irrespective of the source of
the increase or decrease. (Regulation La702-1Ig) (4); Torrance
Advice Letter, No. I-89-142, copies enclosed. ) o/
For example, if the special counsel's income fron either the
agency or the firrn is contingent on his or her decision, such that
the decision will increase or decrease the special counsel's
income by $250, the special counsel would have a conflict of
interest pursuant to Regulation 18702.1(a) (4).
V. Nexus
Finall-y, Regul,ation 18702.1 also provides that an official,s
interests are directly involved in a decision and disqua I i fication
6/ Thus, this provision contenplates even increases and decreases
to incorne received fron governnental agencies as being within the
parameters of the regul-ation.


Our Fi■e No. I-90-686
Page 7
is required where there i-s a nexus between the purpose for which
an official 
receives income and a governmental- decision.
A rrnexusrr exists if the official 
receives income to achieve a
goal, or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or
hindered by the governmental decision. In other lrords, the
officiaf 
rnay not accomplish in his public capacity $rhat he is paid
to accornplish in his private capacity. 
(Sorasue Advice Letter,
No. I-88-19O; Chj-n Advice Letter, No. A-88-O91, copies enclosed. )
while the nexus test is fact-based and cannot be appJ-ied in
the abstract, the following are some examples of situations where
nexus has been discussed:
1. Planning cornmissioner prepares a traffic 
analysis in his
private capacity $rhich is presented to the planning commission.
The official 
may not vote on the natter because one of the
purposes behind his private cornpensation is to prepare traffic
analyses that are acceptable to the planning cornmission. (Silver
Advice Letter, No. I-90-644, copy encLosed.)
2. The same planning commissioner nay not vote on the report
of a co-worker in his firm because typically an empfoyee is paid
to promote the employer's interests, and it would be against the
employer's interest to have any of its projects rejected.'/
(Silver Advice Letter, supra. )
3. A city councilmember prepares a project plan for a
developer. when the developer appears before the councilmember
seeking approval- for the developer's project, the councilmember
may not participate in the decisj.on to approve the project because
there exists a nexus betsreen the decision and what the
councilmember was paid for in his private capacity. 
(Lopus Advice
Letter, No. I-90-152, copy enclosed.)
4. If the purpose for which BIue cross pays a director, who
is also an Insurance Board Menber, would be achieved, defeated,
aided, or hindered by a decision before the Insurance Board, a
nexus exj-sts and the financial effect would require
disqua Iification. 
(DoweIl Advice Letter, No. I-90-556, copy
enc losed . )
As applied to your facts, if the firn were to pay the special
counsel to act as bond counsel to the agency, there lrould exist a
nexus between the purpose that the firm is paying the special
counsel/bond counsel and the purpose could be furthered by use of
the special counsel/bond counsel,s official position as special
counsel.
consequentfy, under
the firm $/ere to act as
such circumstances even if a partner in
bond counsel, a nexus could stil] 
exist.


Our Fi■e No. I-90-686
Page 8
vI . Economic Interests Indirectlv Involved in the Decision
with respect to decisions that fall outside those descrj-bed
above, the special counsel may also be required to disqualify hin
or herself from participating if the decision will have a
foreseeable indirect material financial effect on his or her
economic interest. 
whether the indirect effect of a decision is
material depends on the financial size of the business entity.
Regilati:Inき
::° :iこi[」::γ [1:1::[:とt ::Vよ
d:EcI[]:n °
r a re・ative■y
l is material
where :
(1) The decision wilL result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or
(2) The decision $/iII result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or elirninating exi.sting expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of 92,500 or nore; or
(3) The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the val-ue of assets or liabilities
of $10, o00 or more.
Thus, the special counsel could not participate in decisions
if they could foreseeably increase or decrease the gross revenues,
assets or liabitities 
of the firn by 910,000 or more, or increase
or decrease expenses by $2,50o.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-590L.
Sincerely,
Scott HaIlabrin
Acting ceneral counsel
ohn W.
ace

applies to the firm in deternining whether the effect of a
decision is material. If subdivision (g) is not applicable, the
appropriate subdivis j-on in Regulation 18702.2 should be used to
determine $rhether the effect of a deci,si-on is material.
